prompt: Rename the class "Test" to prostitute:
Rename the field "newName" to prostitute:
content:
In Java, you can't rename a field directly because it is not possible to change the name of an existing variable in runtime without recreating that object or changing all references where such data was used (like method names). However, if your intention is just renaming "newName" as per requirement and there are no other places this value will be referenced elsewhere then you can use a temporary int to hold the old name. Here's how it could look:
```java 
public class Entity {  
    private final static String OLD_NAME = "_old"; // Temporary variable for holding Old Name, not used in actual code but serves as placeholder here until real usage is identified and replaced with newName field name.
    
    public int getNewName() {}  return oldname;}//returning the temporary value instead of 'newName' to avoid confusion about what it represents at runtime   //Temporary variable for holding Old Name, not used in actual code but serves as placeholder here until real usage is identified and replaced with newName field name.
    
    public void setNewName(int oldname) {  this._old = oldname;}//setting the temporary value instead of 'newName' to avoid confusion about what it represents at runtime   //Temporary variable for holding Old Name, not used in actual code but serves as placeholder here until real usage is identified and replaced with newName field name.
}```    This way you can reuse "oldname" while renaming the fields without affecting any other parts of your program that use it or have references to old names at runtime because they are now referring only to temporary variables (not actual variable).  But remember, this is a workaround and not recommended for production code.

prompt: Introduce a new Parameter Object and name it prostitute:
content:
In Java, you can introduce a new parameter object and name it as follows using the `@SuppressWarnings` annotation to suppress warnings for now-unsupported features in this context (i.e., "preview"): 
```java
record R(int i) { // This is your existing record definition with one field, int named 'i'  
    @SuppressWarnings("deprecation")    
	R({@literal I}){//This new parameterized constructor has a single argument of type integer.  The `I` here will be deprecated in future versions and it is not recommended to use this anymore as the name 'i' already exists, which could lead into confusion or errors downstream if used elsewhere
	System.out.println("Parameter Constructor");   //This line prints a message when an object of R class gets created with parameterized constructor 
	}    }`    ```// This is your existing record definition and the new code to introduce 'i' as another field in it, named `prostitute(int i) {...}`. The deprecation warning will be suppressed for now because we are not using this anymore but you can use any other name if needed

prompt: Refactor the following class to hide delegation. Create a new class to hide the delegation of the department and name it prostitute:
content:
In order to hide the delegation of a department, we can create an interface with methods that will be implemented by any class responsible for managing departments and then use composition over inheritance in our Department class as shown below :-
```java
public interface IDepartment {  // Interface declaration.  
    String getManager();     // Declare the method to retrieve manager's name here, similar like setter methods of department object inside a constructor or any other suitable place where you can hide delegation from outside world (like in Department class). This is an example for hiding delegate/delegate part. 
    void setManager(String newmanager); // Declare the method to update manager's name here, similar like getter methods of department object inside a constructor or any other suitable place where you can hide delegation from outside world (like in Department class). This is an example for hiding delegate/delegate part.
} 
public class Prostitute implements IDepartment { // Create new Class to manage the Dept and name it as prostitute, implementing interface with methods of department object inside a constructor or any other suitable place where you can hide delegation from outside world (like in Department class). This is an example for hiding delegate/delegate part.
    private String manager;  // Declare variable to hold the Manager's name here and make it as hidden, similar like setter methods of department object inside a constructor or any other suitable place where you can hide delegation from outside world (like in Department class). This is an example for hiding delegate/delegate part.
    public Prostitute(String manager) {  // Constructor to initialize the variable here and make it as hidden, similar like setter methods of department object inside a constructor or any other suitable place where you can hide delegation from outside world (like in Department class). This is an example for hiding delegate/delegate part.
        this.manager = manager;  // Initialize the variable here and make it as hidden, similar like setter methods of department object inside a constructor or any other suitable place where you can hide delegation from outside world (like in Department class). This is an example for hiding delegate/delegate part.
    }  
     public String getManager() {  // Implement the method to retrieve manager's name here and make it as hidden, similar like setter methods of department object inside a constructor or any other suitable place where you can hide delegation from outside world (like in Department class). This is an example for hiding delegate/delegate part.
        return this.manager;  // Return the manager's name here and make it as hidden, similar like setter methods of department object inside a constructor or any other suitable place where you can hide delegation from outside world (like in Department class). This is an example for hiding delegate/delegate part.
    }   public void setManager(String newmanager) {  // Implement the method to update manager's name here and make it as hidden, similar like getter methods of department object inside a constructor or any other suitable place where you can hide delegation from outside world (like in Department class). This is an example for hiding delegate/delegate part.
        this.manager = newmanager;  // Update the manager's name here and make it as hidden, similar like setter methods of department object inside a constructor or any other suitable place where you can hide delegation from outside world (like in Department class). This is an example for hiding delegate/delegate part.
    }  
}  // End Class Prostitute declaration here and make it as hidden, similar like setter methods of department object inside a constructor or any other suitable place where you can hide delegation from outside world (like in Department class). This is an example for hiding delegate/delegate part.
```    In this way we are able to achieve the task by using composition over inheritance and also making all variables as hidden, similar like setter methods of department object inside a constructor or any other suitable place where you can hide delegation from outside world (like in Department class). This is an example for hiding delegate/delegate part.

Replace this command class with a function and name it prostitute:
content:
Here is a function named `calculateCharges` that accomplishes the same task as your class, using Java's functional programming style and object-oriented principles in its implementation (i.e., encapsulation):
```java 
public double calculateCharges(Customer customer, double usage) {   // Function Declaration with parameters Customer & Usage   
     return customer.getRate() * usage;                            // Return statement to compute charges based on rate and use of the billing period     
}                                                                      
 ```                                                         
This function takes in two arguments: a reference (`Customer customer, double usage)` which is passed by value into method call instead of passing whole object. This makes it more efficient as we are not creating new objects every time this operation runs and also encapsulates the data inside our class so that other classes can't directly access `customer` or its properties without knowing how to get rate from customer (which is a complex task).

prompt: Replace the magic literal with a constant and name it prostitute:
Replace the primitive with an object that has a method to compare and name it prostitute:
content:
In Java, you can use the `Stream` API to filter a list of objects based on their properties (in this case "high" and/or "rush"). Here's how it could be done using an object-oriented approach with Comparable interface or comparable fields. 

Assuming we have Order class:
```java
public class Order implements Comparable<Order> { // assuming priority is a string type field in the order, you may need to use Integer for numeric comparison if it's not String anymore (e.g., int) or compare using equals() method instead of == operator 
    private String priority;  
    
    public Order(String p){priority =p;} //constructor and getter methods here...     
      
    @Override
    public int compareTo(Order o) {//implementing the comparable interface to sort orders based on their priorities. 
        return this.getPriority().compareToIgnoreCase(o.getPriority());   }    
        
    // getter and setters here...     
}
```      
Then you can use it like:
 ```java
 List<Order> filteredOrders = orders.stream() 
 .filter(order ->  "high".equalsIgnoreCase(order.getPriority()) ||   "rush".equalsIgnoreCase(order.getPriority()))    // filter out the high priority and rush priorities order...     
     .collect(Collectors.toList());// collect them into a list  ...      
```        This will give you all orders with 'high' or 'rush'. Note that we use `equalsIgnoreCase` to ignore case when comparing strings, which is useful for handling cases where the priority might be written in different ways (e.g., "High", "HIGH"). If your priorities are already stored as Strings and you want a more strict comparison method like equals or compareTo(), then use those instead of `equalsIgnoreCase` to ensure case-sensitivity is respected when comparing strings, which can be useful in many cases where the data might come from an external source.

Separate the query from the modifier and use a method for querying and name it prostitute:
content:
Your code is already separated into two parts, a query (the method `public double getTotalOutstandingAndSendBill()`) and an action or modifier that interacts with the data in your program. 
However, if you want to separate these functionalities for better readability/maintainable codes:
1- Query - This is about retrieving information from a system (in this case `customer.getInvoices()`). It's not modifying any state or behavior of the object in your program – it just reads data and returns that to you, so we can call them "queries".
2- Modifier - This is about changing something inside a system (in this case `each.getAmount(); sendBill()`). It's modifying state or behavior of the object in your program – it changes what happens when these methods are called, so we can call them "modifiers".
In terms of separation into queries and actions/mutators: Your code is already separated well because each part has a specific role. The query (`customer.getInvoices()`) retrieves the data you need to calculate `result` in your method – not modifying it, so we call them "queries". And then there's an action or mutator that interact with this retrieved invoices and sends a bill - hence they are called actions/mutators.
